stem: A hospital uses a CT scan screening to screen for early-stage lung cancer. The test has a sensitivity of 78% and a specificity of 86%. The prevalence of early-stage lung cancer in the patient population is estimated to be between 8.9% and 16.6%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has early-stage lung cancer? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(early-stage lung cancer | positive test) ranges from 0.352 to 0.526 as prevalence varies from 8.9% to 16.6%. Point estimate at 12.8% prevalence: 0.45.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.14.
The confidence interval [0.352, 0.526] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (78%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
